coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted sustainable synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots for selective sensing of Hg (II) ions and cancer cell imaging

coredata.dc:description: Microwave-assisted synthesis of water-soluble yellow emissive carbon dots (YECDs) of ∼2.8 nm size has been synthesized using m-phenylenediamine and malonic acid. YECDs show excitation-independent emission properties and display the maximum fluorescence intensity at 550 nm when excited at 420 nm excitation with a high quantum yield of ∼21 %. The water-soluble YECDs are used for selective sensing of Hg2+ metal ions in aqueous media even in the presence of other interfering metal ions with a good limit of detection of ∼191 nM. For field application purposes, the YECDs-coated paper strips are used for selective sensing Hg2+ ions. In addition, YECDs were successfully used to measure the content of Hg2+ metal ions in potable water and industrial wastewater samples. Finally, we have proposed the quenching mechanism of YECDs in the presence of Hg2+, which occurred via both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms through the interaction of Hg2+ ions with the functional group present in YECDs. Moreover, highly emissive, low-toxic YECDs are used for cancer cell imaging under blue light and can be further developed for the subcellular targeting probe.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Representation of Microwave-Assisted Synthesis of Yellow Emissive Carbon Dots (YECDs).

(a) Normalized UV-visible spectra (brown), excitation spectra, and emission spectra of YECDs. Inset digital pictures of an aqueous solution of YECDs under UV-light (right) and daylight (left). (b) Excitation-independent emission spectra of YECDs (0.05 mg mL−1). (c) TEM image of YECDs. (d) The corresponding size distribution histogram of YECDs. (e) HR-TEM image of YECDs shows the graphitic fringes.

The XPS study of YECDs. (a) Percentage of elemental composition (inset image) and full survey scan spectra. (b-d) Deconvoluted spectra of C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s, showing the different bindings present in YECDs.

Selective sensing of Hg2+ metal ions using YECDs. (a) Fluorescence spectra of YECDs (0.05 mg mL−1) under 420 nm excitation before and after the addition of different metal ions at a 3.33 mM concentration. (b) The relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) of YECDs before and after the addition of different metal ions. (c) The digital image of YECDs solution in the absence and presence of metal ions under UV light (d) The digital image of YECDs coated paper strips in the absence and presence of metal ions under UV light.

Normalized absorption spectra of Hg2+ ions, excitation and emission spectra of YECDs. (b) The UV–vis absorbance spectra of YECDs before and after the addition of all tested metal ions. (c) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of YECDs. (d) The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of YECDs after the addition of Hg2+ ions (3.33 mM). The concentration of YECDs is 0.05 mg mL−1 for all the above-mentioned experiments.

(a) Fluorescence titration spectra of YECDs in addition to varying concentrations of Hg2+ ions. (b) Stern-Volmer linear fitting curve of the Hg2+ ions concentration against the relative fluorescence intensity (I0/I) of YECDs at 550 nm. (c) A Schematic representation of how Hg2+Metal ion is sensed through interaction with functional groups on YECDs surface.

(a) The bar diagram displays the PL intensity ratio (I/I0) of YECDs with/without Hg2+ ions in the presence of other interfering metal ions at a concentration of 3.33 mM. (b) A bar plot of the PL intensity ratio (I/I0) of YECDs in the presence of all non-quenching metal ions, and with Hg2+ ions in the presence of all non-quenching metal ions.
(a) YECDs (0.4 mg mL−1) labeled cancerous KB cells. (b) MTT-based cell viability study.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted sustainable synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots for selective sensing of Hg (II) ions and cancer cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microwave-assisted synthesis of water-soluble yellow emissive carbon dots (YECDs) of ∼2.8 nm size has been synthesized using m-phenylenediamine and malonic acid. YECDs show excitation-independent emission properties and display the maximum fluorescence intensity at 550 nm when excited at 420 nm excitation with a high quantum yield of ∼21 %. The water-soluble YECDs are used for selective sensing of Hg2+ metal ions in aqueous media even in the presence of other interfering metal ions with a good limit of detection of ∼191 nM. For field application purposes, the YECDs-coated paper strips are used for selective sensing Hg2+ ions. In addition, YECDs were successfully used to measure the content of Hg2+ metal ions in potable water and industrial wastewater samples. Finally, we have proposed the quenching mechanism of YECDs in the presence of Hg2+, which occurred via both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms through the interaction of Hg2+ ions with the functional group present in YECDs. Moreover, highly emissive, low-toxic YECDs are used for cancer cell imaging under blue light and can be further developed for the subcellular targeting probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered much attention due to their remarkable electrical [1], and tunable optical properties [2,3]. There are two major ways to synthesize CDs: top-down and bottom-up approaches [4]. Larger carbon structures are broken down into CDs in a top-down procedure while small CDs are constructed from molecular precursors using bottom-up processes [5]. Generally, top-down approaches encompass a variety of processes such as chemical oxidation [6], laser ablation [7], electrochemical oxidation [8], etc. The bottom-up techniques typically involve hydrothermal [9], solvothermal [10], ultrasonic treatment [11], thermal decomposition [12], and microwave-assisted [13,14] synthesis techniques. One of the useful bottom-up methods for synthesizing CDs is microwave-assisted pyrolysis, which involves utilizing high microwave radiation to break chemical bonds [15]. The most practical method is now microwave-assisted pyrolysis because of its better product yield, shorter reaction time, simplicity of technique, sustainability, and usage of efficient energy [16]. In addition, this method produces homogeneous heating in a shorter amount of time, leading to the formation of CDs with uniform size. The fluorescence property of CDs can be tuned from the UVvisible to the near-infrared (NIR) region of the spectrum by changing the size of the particle, initial precursors, and the functional groups present on the surface of the particle [17,18]. Typically, primary amine, hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and amide functional groups lie on the surface of CDs are able to provide brighter fluorescence of CDs [19]. In addition, elemental doping (N, B, F, S, and P) can also tune the fluorescence property of CDs [17,18,20]. Recent development of fluorescent CDs using microwave such as CdTe/CdS@ZnS–SiO2 quantum dots (QDs) [21], CDs [13], nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) [14,22–25]. Fluorescent CDs have been developed over the years and used in several fields including bioimaging [13,26], photocatalysis [27], electrocatalysis [28], sensing [9], and anti-counterfeiting [29] because of their brighter fluorescence, high photostability, and non-toxicity [30,31].

The concerns about human health and environmental safety in recent years, the scientific community has focused more on the development of extremely selective and sensitive fluorescent carbon-based nanoprobes as heavy metal ions sensors [32]. Mercury (Hg2+) ions are the most common heavy metal ions and are a type of lethal metal ions that are found in many different environments, particularly in water across the globe. Owing to its bioaccumulation and direct toxicity, Hg2+ causes environmental pollution, renal disease, immune system disorders, and other illnesses in both people and animals even at trace levels [33]. Several conventional techniques have been employed to detect the Hg2+ heavy metal ions such as electrochemical techniques [34], voltammetry [35], mass spectrometry [36], colorimetric analysis [37], fluorometric analysis [14,38], and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [39]. The majority of the approaches are laborious, need expensive instruments, and are time-consuming [40]. However, according to developments in the field, the fluorometric detection methodology has been employed as a simple and affordable method [41]. Recent fluorescent nanoprobes which are used for Hg2+ sensing such as CdTe/CdS@ZnS–SiO2 QDs [21], nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) [14,22–25,42–44], CDs [13,45–54] and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (NS-CDs) [55,56]. Among other fluorescent nanoprobes, carbon-based nanoprobes especially have attracted more interest to researchers because they have colloidal stability, tunable optical properties, photostability, low-toxicity, and selectivity/sensitivity toward specific metal ions [1–3,30,31].

Here, we have synthesized water-soluble, low-toxic yellow emissive carbon dots (YECDs) using a simple, one-step microwave technique. YECDs are obtained by amidation of malonic acid and m-phenylenediamine followed by carbonization. The primary amine, carboxylic acid, and amide-terminated YECDs are used for selective sensing of Hg2+ ions even in the presence of other interfering metal ions in aqueous media. For field application purposes, the YECDs-coated paper strips are used for selective sensing Hg2+ ions. YECDs have the potential to detect Hg2+ ions with a very low detection limit. Interestingly, YECDs are used for the determination of Hg2+ ions present in real potable water and industrial wastewater samples. Based on our findings, we proposed that the sensing of Hg2+ ions occurs via both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms through the interaction of Hg2+ ions with the functional group lying on the surface of YECDs. In addition, the low-toxic, water-soluble YECDs are used for cancer cell imaging under blue light.

Materials and Reagents. Malonic acid, m-phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the metal salts such as magnesium nitrate, copper nitrate, mercuric acetate, cadmium nitrate, zinc nitrate, lead nitrate, nickel nitrate, ferrous chloride, calcium nitrate, cobalt nitrate, and magneses nitrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as it is. Ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA), sodium bicarbonate, sodium carbonate, boric acid, borax, sodium monohydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium acetate, and glacial acetic acid used for buffer solutions, were purchased from Central Drug House (CDH). Fluorescamine, methyl thiazolyl diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and sodium dodecyl sulfate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The Whatman paper was used for paper strip-based study. For every experiment, we used deionized water with the following characteristics: pH: ∼6.9, conductivity: 16 µS/cm, and TDS: 3.2 ppm.

Instrumentation. A Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer LS 55 spectrophotometer and HORIBA Fluoromax-4 spectrophotometer. Analysis of FT-IR Spectrum was done using FT-IR Bruker Alpha II Equipment. HR-TEM 200 Kev was used to examine the morphology. To analyze the elemental composition, PHl 5000 Versa Probe lll equipment was utilized for XPS measurements. To record the fluorescence lifetime decay curves, an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 Time-Resolved Photoluminescence or Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TRPL or TCSPC) spectrophotometer (excitation at 420 nm and emission 550 nm) was employed. The fluorescence images of the cells were taken using an Olympus IX 81 microscope and Image-Pro Plus v 7.0 software. The hydrodynamic size of YECDs was measured using a Malvern Nano ZS apparatus.

Synthesis of Microwave-Assisted Yellow Emissive Carbon Dots (YECDs). Microwave-assisted yellow emissive carbon dots (YECDs) were synthesized using m-phenylenediamine and malonic acid through amidation followed by a carbonization process [57]. In brief, 250 mg of m-phenylenediamine and 250 mg of malonic acid were taken in a 30 ml round bottom reaction vessel and 10 ml water was added to this. Next, this mixture was sonicated for a few minutes to dissolve the reagents and placed in the microwave (Monomode CEM Discover MW). Next, the reaction was continued for 10–15 min at 180ºC temperature and ∼10 bar pressure under 40 W. The dark brown color solution indicated the production of carbon dots (CDs). For control synthesis, only 500 mg of m-phenylenediamine/p-phenylenediamine/o-phenylenediamine was taken in 10 ml water and followed the same procedure. Similarly, only 500 mg of malonic acid was used for control synthesis, and no brown color was observed. The crude brown-colored solution was purified through dialysis using a cellulose membrane (MWCO ∼ 4 KDa). Finally, the purified sample 1.0 mg mL−1 was used for further characterization and applications. An earlier method used quinine sulfate as a reference to determine the quantum yield (QY) of the YECDs sample. Quinine sulfate was used as a standard by considering the quantum yield of the quinine sulfate to be 54 % [13,22,23,58] to determine the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of YECDs, which was found to be 21 %.

Confirmation of the Presence of Primary Amine on the Surface of YECDs. The primary amine group present on the surface of YECDs is verified by the fluorescamine test [9,59]. This test, as previously described, was used to confirm that primary amines were present on the surface of the nanoprobe. In brief, 50 μL of YECDs solution (0.01 mg mL−1) was added to 50 μL of buffer solution of pH 9, and 200 μL of acetone solution of fluorescamine (0.02 M) was added. The fluorescence spectra were recorded before and after the addition of fluorescamine to confirm the primary amine group is present on the surface of YECDs. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 483 nm by exciting at 400 nm. The linear equation of the calibration curve as previously reported Y = 0.0002X was used to calculate primary amine concentration [59].

Sensing Experiments. At first, for selective sensing investigation, 20 mM concentrations of various metal ions solutions including Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, and Mn2+ were prepared. In brief, 400 μL of the prepared each metal ion solution was added to 2 mL of 0.05 mg mL−1 YECDs solution in a vial. Afterward, fluorescence spectra of YECDs were captured at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, in the absence and presence of metal ions. For interference study, non-quenching metal ions (Mg2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, and Mn2+) were added to YECDs solution followed by addition of Hg2+ ions and fluorescence spectra were captured under excitation at 420 nm. In addition, fluorescence spectra of YECDs after all metal ions followed by the addition of Hg2+ ions were recorded. To find out the limit of detection (LOD), fluorescence spectra of YECDs solution followed by addition of Hg2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–0.048 mM were recorded. To check the pH-dependent fluorescence property of YECDs, fluorescence spectra were recorded after maintaining the pH of the solution using a buffer solution. In brief, acetate buffer, PBS buffer, borate buffer, and bicarbonate buffer were used to maintain pH∼4.6, pH∼7.4, pH∼9, and pH∼10 respectively. For further investigation, a 400 µL of 20 mM solution of Hg2+ was added to all buffer solutions of pH∼4.6, pH∼7.4, pH∼9, and pH∼10. Next, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength of 420 nm.

Real Sample Analysis. Wastewater samples were collected from industries near NIT Delhi and used to explore the real application of YECDs. In brief, all samples including potable water and wastewater samples were spiked with a 200 nM Hg2+ metal ions solution and used for recovery experiments. Next, using the usual method listed below, the percentages of Hg2+ recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were determined [13,60].

Selective Sensing of Hg2+ ion Using Paper Strip. For paper strip method, at first, the cellulose Whatman paper was cut into 4 cm × 2 cm strips and soaked it in YECDs solution for 10–15 min, and allowed to dry at room temperature for 15–30 min [13,14]. After that, the YECDs-soaked fluorescent paper strips were immersed (up to half-length of the paper strip) in all tested-metal salt solution and dried it. The digital images of the paper strips were captured under daylight and UV light.

Life-time measurement experiment. To calculate the fluorescence lifetime of YECDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, 400 μL of 20 mM solution of Hg2+ was added to 2 mL of 0.05 mg mL−1 YECDs solution and used for Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TRPL).

Cancer Cell Imaging and Cytotoxicity Study. Based on an earlier report, KB cancer cells were grown [9,61]. Subsequently, 50–100 μL of the sample (0.05 mg mL−1) were added to the cells, and they were incubated for 1–2 h. Then, a fresh medium was added after washing with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to get rid of any floating debris and unbound samples. Finally, the washed cells were used for fluorescence imaging.

For the cytotoxicity investigation of YECDs, KB cells were utilized [22,61]. Cells were first cultured in 24-well plates as per the reported method. After that, YECDs were added in various concentrations (0.2–1.0 mg mL−1) and left for 24 h. After that, 50 μL of freshly made MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added to the washed cells, and they were left to stand for 4 h. The formazan crystal was then left on the plate after the remaining solution was carefully removed. After dissolving the violet-colored crystal in a sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) solution (4 g of SDS dissolved in 20 mL of a DMF-H2O mixture), absorbance was measured at 570 nm. The estimation of cell viability was based on the assumption that 100 % of untreated control cells would survive.

Synthesis and Characterization of Microwave-Assisted Yellow Emissive Carbon Dots (YECDs). Water-soluble YECDs have been synthesized through the rapid microwave pyrolysis method using a mixture of malonic acid and m-phenylenediamine, Scheme 1 . After 10–15 min of reaction, a dark brown color solution was observed, indicating the formation of carbon dots (CDs). In microwave, CDs are formed via an amidation reaction of malonic acid and m-phenylenediamine followed by carbonization. Purified CDs show yellow emission under UV light. In addition, we performed the same reaction using only m-phenylenediamine and using the mixture of o-phenylenediamine/p-phenylenediamine with malonic acid. Fluorescence spectra indicate that the combination of m-phenylenediamine and malonic acid shows higher fluorescence intensity due to the new extended conjugated structure formed between them [57], and for the incorporation of carboxylic acid and amide functional group, Supporting Information Figure S1. To determine the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of YECDs, quinine sulfate was used as a standard, which was found to be 21 %. The quantum yield was calculated using the reported formula [58], is as follows-

where (QY)S and (QY)R indicate the quantum yield of the sample and reference; IS and IR represent the area under the fluorescence curve of sample and reference; AR and AS represent the absorbance value of reference and sample; ηS and ηR represent the refractive index of the solvent for sample and reference respectively.

To investigate their optical characteristics, we employed UV-visible spectroscopy, revealing absorption maxima at 231 and 283 which are associated with the π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bond and graphitic C = C bonds respectively [61–63]. The peak at 424 nm leads to the n-π* transition of C = O bond, Fig. 1 a [61,64]. The aqueous solution of YECDs exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity at 550 nm upon excitation at a wavelength of 420 nm. Moreover, YECDs excitation spectra were recorded at an emission wavelength of around 550 nm and the excitation center was observed at ∼420 nm. A digital picture of colloidally stable YECDs solution (prepared 4 months ago) under UV light (right) and daylight (left), shows the colloidal stability and yellow emission of YECDs, inset of Fig. 1a. Next, the fluorescence spectra of YECDs were taken under a variable excitation wavelength of 380–480 nm, confirming the excitation-independent emission property, Fig. 1b. As compared to commonly synthesized CDs, this kind of phenomenon is unique and it explains the uniform size and homogeneous surface passivation of YECDs [23,57,61]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to examine the shape and diameter of YECDs. The homogeneous dispersion and spherical morphology of the YECDs are depicted in the low-resolution TEM image, Fig. 1c. The average diameter of YECDs is found to be ∼2.8 nm, as indicated by the size distribution histogram, Fig. 1d. The fringes of graphitic planes are observed in the high-resolution TEM image of YECDs, Fig. 1e. The inter-planar fringes are 0.26 nm apart on average, according to the d-spacing, indicating the [002] graphitic plane [65,66]. A study using dynamic light scattering (DLS) revealed that the hydrodynamic size of colloidal YECDs is around 16–21 nm, Supporting Information Figure S2. The hydrodynamic size of YECDs comprises the condensed core of YECDs, which is about ∼2.8 nm in size and capped with different types of functional groups that lead to water solubility of YECDs. By contrasting the hydrodynamic size of YECDs with TEM data, we may conclude that the particle possess a spherical shape having ∼2.8 nm condensed core with ∼13.2–18.2 nm of soft shell [67]. In addition, we have performed DLS study of aqueous solution of YECDs after leaving the solution for a month, Supporting Information Figure S2. Results show that no significant change in hydrodynamic size is observed, also indicating the good colloidal stability of YECDs.

Elemental Composition, different types of chemical bonding, and kind of functional groups on the surface of YECDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Three prominent peaks at about ∼530, ∼396.2 and ∼282 eV correspond to the O1 s, N1 s, and C1 s elements respectively, are observed in Fig. 2 a [63]. It is found that the elemental composition of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen is ∼77.92 %, ∼20.01 %, and ∼2.07 % respectively. Three peaks are seen in the deconvoluted spectra of C1 s at 282, 283.04, and 286.9 eV corresponding to the CC/C = C, CO/CN, and C = O/C = N bond respectively, Fig. 2b [58,63,68]. According to the deconvolution spectra of N1 s, two peaks are observed at 398.6 and 399.7 eV, corresponding to the C = N and NH bond, Fig. 2c [14,69]. Two peaks are observed in the deconvoluted spectra of O1s at 530.8 and 532.1 eV corresponding to the C = O bonds and CO bonds respectively, Fig. 2d [14].

The functional groups present on the surface of YECDs were further confirmed by FT-IR analysis, Supporting Information Figure S3. The peaks at 3314 and 3183 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the NH bond of primary amines in m-phenylenediamine [57]. These two peaks are observed in YECDs at 3365 and 3175 cm−1, indicating the presence of the primary amine group on the surface of YECDs by comparing the spectra with m-phenylenediamine [9,22]. The peaks in YECDs at 1701 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 correspond to C = O stretching frequency of the carboxylic acid and amide group present on the surface of YECDs [13,57,68]. The new peak at 1575 cm− 1 is observed in YECDs for C = C/C = N bond, confirming the carbonization process [14]. Next, a fluorescamine test is performed to ensure that the primary amine group is present on the surface of YECDs [9]. Under 400 nm excitation, the fluorescamine-treated YECDs exhibit a higher emission at 483 nm in comparison to the control, suggesting the existence of primary amine functional groups in YECDs, Supporting Information Figure S4. Using the previously published standard calibration curve, we have calculated the concentration of primary amines in YECDs which is found to be 0.05 μM [59].

Selective Sensing of Hg2+ Metal Ions in Aqueous Media and In Paper Strip Using YECDs. YECDs were employed for selective sensing of Hg2+ ions in aqueous solution. For this study, a series of metal comprising Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, and Mn2+ is employed. To explore the selectivity of YECDs, fluorescence spectra of YECDs are collected under excitation at 420 nm upon the addition of the above-mentioned metal ions to a solution of YECDs. The quenching of fluorescence intensity of YECDs is observed in the case of Hg2+ metal ions, Fig. 3 a. The relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 also shows that the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly with the addition of Hg2+ metal ions, Fig. 3b. Digital images of YECDs with and without metal ions under daylight as well as UV light indicate the selective fluorescence quenching of YECDs for Hg2+ metal ions, Fig. 3c and Supporting Information Figures S5. No aggregation is observed after addition of Hg2+ metal ions in the YECDs solution, which is also shown in digital images. To develope the paper strip-based sensor for field application, firstly we have checked the fluorescence stability of the paper strip, Supporting Information Figures S6. The fluorescent digital images of YECDs soaked paper strips under UV light after one month of YECDs soaking, indicate the fluorescence stability of the paper strips. Then, we explored the paper strip-based selective sensing of Hg2+ ions among other divalent metal ions, Fig. 3d and Supporting Information Figures S7. Typically, YECDs-coated Whatman cellulose filter papers were used for sensing of Hg2+ ions. Results show that yellow emission of YECDs coated paper strip under UV light is significantly quenched in the presence of Hg2+ ions, Fig. 3d. Under daylight, the color of the yellow paper strip slightly changes for Hg2+ ions and remains the same for other metal ions, Supporting Information Figures S7.

Plausible Sensing Mechanism of Hg2+ Ions Using YECDs. The fluorescence quenching of YECDs in addition to Hg2+ ions can be described by different types of fluorescence quenching mechanisms [32,70]. In general, the inner filter effect (IFE), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching, and static quenching are the primary quenching mechanisms [62]. For FRET, PET, and IFE to quench the fluorescence, there must be a certain amount of overlap between the absorption spectra of the quencher and the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophore [61]. To investigate the mechanism, normalized absorption spectra of Hg2+ along with excitation and emission spectra of YECDs are plotted, Fig. 4 a. The absorption spectra of Hg2+ do not overlap with the excitation/emission spectra of YECDs, indicating no possibility of the IFE, FRET, and PET mechanism [9,56,61,71] Next, we have taken UV-visible absorbance spectra of YECDs before and after the addition of the all tested metal ions, Fig. 4b. The change in absorbance is observed after the addition of Hg2+ metal ions, indicating the possibility of static quenching [9,13]. Further, time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of YECDs are taken without and with Hg2+ ions, Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d. The average lifetime is computed using a biexponential fitting of the spectra with a satisfactory correlation coefficient (R2 > 0.997). It was observed that the average lifetime of YECDs without and with Hg2+ ions are 1.58 ns and 0.092 ns respectively. The outcomes demonstrate that the addition of Hg2+ ions significantly alters the lifetime of YECDs, indicating the occurrence of dynamic quenching of YECDs on addition of Hg2+ ions [20,51,64]. Therefore, we may conclude that the fluorescence quenching of YECDs in the presence of Hg2+ ions is caused by both static and dynamic quenching process [62,72]. Moreover, the decreasing lifetime after the addition of Hg2+ also confirms the heavy atom effect which amplifies the collisional fluorescence quenching [73].

Next, we carried out a fluorescence titration to confirm the occurrence of both static and dynamic quenching mechanism and to estimate the limit of detection (LOD) of Hg2+ metal ions utilizing YECDs as a fluorescent sensing probe [72]. The findings show that, when the Hg2+ ions concentration rises, the fluorescence intensity of YECDs decreases, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. Next, in the concentration range of 0–0.048 mM, the relative fluorescence intensity I0/I as a function of the concentration of Hg2+ ions is plotted, where I is the fluorescence intensity of YECDs in the presence of Hg2+ and I0 is the fluorescence intensity of YECDs in the absence of Hg2+ metal ions at 550 nm wavelength. Fig. 5b shows that the spectrum is an upward curvature, concave towards the y-axis, confirming the occurrence of both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms after the addition of Hg2+ metal ions [73,74]. Thus, the modified Stren-Volmer equation which accounts for upward curvature observed for both static and dynamic quenching occurs as follows-

Where, I0 and I is the fluorescence intensity of YECDs in absence and presence of a quencher. KD and KS are the dynamic and static Stern-Volmer constant. It is also observed that the initial range of concentration the Stern-Volmer plot is found to be linear with an outstanding correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.999, Fig. 5b. Finally, LOD is also determined using the previously reported equation [73] and is found to be 191 nM.

The dynamic quenching occurs by the collision in the excited state when Hg2+ ions interact with functional groups present on the surface of YECDs [13,51,62,64]. Additionally, to investigate how functional groups present on the surface of YECDs interact with Hg2+ ions, we performed FTIR investigation before and after the addition of Hg2+ ions, Supporting Information Figure S8. The peak at 3365 cm−1 becomes broader in the presence of Hg2+ metal ions, suggesting the interaction of the -NH2 functional group from the surface of YECDs with Hg2+ metal ions. Interestingly, the peak at 1701 cm−1 for C = O stretching of the -COOH functional group in YECDs drastically diminished in the presence of Hg2+ ions, indicating the interaction of Hg2+ ions with -COOH functional groups. Additionally, we have studied the zeta potential of YECDs before and after addition of Hg2+ metal ions. The zeta-potential value changes from −11 mV to +33 mV in addition to Hg2+ metal ions, suggesting the interaction of -COOH functional groups with Hg2+ ions. To further confirm our suggestion, fluorescence spectra are recorded before and after the addition of 10 mM solutions of metal ions chelator ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) to the solution of YECDs containing Hg2+ ions, Supporting Information Figure S9. The result shows that the quenched fluorescence intensity of YECDs is partially recovered in addition to EDTA, suggesting that the metal ions chelator EDTA is unable to overcome the binding of YECDs with Hg2+ ions and also implies that YECDs strongly interact with Hg2+ ions [20]. Based on our results, we may conclude that both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms occur when Hg2+ ions interact with the functional groups present on the surface of YECDs, Fig. 5c.

A comparative investigation of several carbon-based fluorescent sensing probes for the sensing of Hg2+ metal ions is summarized in a tabular form, showing the efficiency of YECDs as a sensor as well as an imaging probe, Table S1, Supporting Information. Mostly, blue emissive carbon-based nanoprobes have been synthesized using microwave techniques and used for Hg2+ ion sensing even though a few of them had comparatively lower LOD values. Additionally, several of the nanoprobes also have problems with selectivity, demand more time in their production, and are not economical. Here, we used sustainable microwave techniques to produce YECDs and used for sensing and imaging probe.

Impact of Interfering Metal Ions on the Selective Sensing of Hg2+ Ions. The impact of interfering metal ions during selective sensing of Hg2+ metal ions using YECDs is performed, Fig. 6 a and Supporting Information Figure S10. Typically, non-quenching Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Co2+, and Mn2+ metal ions are added to the aqueous solution of YECDs, and then Hg2+ metal ions are added in each non-quenching metal ions containing YECDs solution. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of YECDs are recorded and the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 shows that the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly with the addition of Hg2+ metal ions even in the presence of interfering metal ions, Fig. 6a. Subsequently, the selective fluorescence quenching of YECDs toward Hg2+ metal ions in the presence of all non-quenching metal ions together is also studied, Fig. 6b and Supporting Information Figure S11. The fluorescence of YECDs is observed to be quenched upon the addition of Hg2+ ions even in the presence of all other interfering metal ions. The results demonstrate the ability of YECDs as sensors even in complex systems. Additionally, we have performed Hg2+ metal ions sensing experiments in the presence of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) [75,76] including glycine, urea, and phenol, Supporting Information Figure S12. Results show that in the presence of the above-mentioned organic molecules, the fluorescence of YECDs is quenched upon the addition of Hg2+ ions. Results conclude the selective sensing of Hg2+ ions even in the presence of organic molecules.

Effect of pH During Sensing of Hg2+ Metal Ions and Real Application of the Sensor. The effect of pH on Hg2+ metal ions sensing in aqueous solution was investigated. At first, we checked the effect of pH on the fluorescence of YECDs at pH = 4.6, 7.4, 9.0, and 10.0, Supporting Information Figure S13. Results show that there is no significant change in fluorescence intensity at pH = 7.4, 9.0, and 10.0, and fluorescence intensity significantly decreases at pH = 4.6. Thus, we may conclude that the fluorescence is stable in the pH range between ∼ 7–10. At lower pH ∼ 4, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreases, which may be due to the variation of surface charge of YECDs due to the protonation of the functional group [64,77,78]. Next, we used YECDs to detect Hg2+ metal ions at various pH levels. In addition, Hg2+ metal ions cause fluorescence quenching of YECDs at different pH, indicating the potential of YECDs as a sensor over a broad pH scale and can be widely used for real applications in complex systems, Supporting Information Figure S14. In addition, YECDs were successfully used to measure the content of Hg2+ metal ions in potable water and industrial wastewater samples. For this purpose, 200 nM Hg2+ metal ion solution was spiked in all real samples and used for recovery study [56,60,79]. Next, we have estimated the content of Hg2+ ions in real samples after spiking of Hg2+ metal ions externally. Table 1 compiles the Hg2+ contents that were found after spiking of Hg2+ metal ions in real samples. The relative standard deviation (RSD) and percentage of Hg2+ recovery provide evidence of the efficiency of our sensing nanoprobe.

YECDs for Cancer Cells Imaging and Cytotoxicity Study. We performed a cell imaging experiment in KB cells to investigate the capability of YECDs as an imaging probe. The cell image under blue light exposure demonstrates the substantial labeling of YECDs in KB cancer cells, Fig. 7 a. There is no need for further functionalization as the surface of the YECDs is covered with primary amine groups to interact with the cell membrane [59,61,80]. Moreover, a cytotoxicity study using methyl thiazolyl diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was conducted in KB cells using varying concentrations of YECDs ranging from 0.2 to 1.0 mg mL−1, Fig. 7b. YECDs have great cell viability up to a significant dose of 1.0 mg mL−1 where we used 0.4 mg mL−1 concentration for cell imaging experiment. Therefore, low-toxic YECDs can be used as a cancer cell imaging probe.

CONCLUSIONS. In conclusion, we synthesized single-step microwave-assisted yellow emissive carbon dots (YECDs) and used them for selective sensing of Hg2+ ions and cancer cell imaging. The yellow emission of YECDs is quenched in the presence of Hg2+ metal ions without interference from other metal ions in aqueous solutions. For field application purposes, the YECDs-coated paper strips are used for selective sensing Hg2+ ions. The heavy metal ions Hg2+ can be detected with a low limit of detection and it can be used as a sensor in a wide pH range. Interestingly, low-toxic water-soluble YECDs were also used for cancer cell imaging under blue light and can be further developed for the subcellular targeting probe. For a real application, YECDs were successfully used to measure the content of Hg2+ metal ions in potable water and industrial wastewater samples. Based on our findings, we suggest that the YECDs interact with Hg2+ metal ions through the functional groups lying on the surface of the probe, and the quenching of fluorescence occurs via both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms. Based on these qualities, YECDs may find additional utility in the future.
